content: A non-alcoholic fatty liver is characterized by excess triglycerides and fat in the liver, leading to a fatty liver.
De novo lipogenesis is triggered by excessive carbohydrate consumption, particularly fructose, which is converted into fat in the liver.
The ketogenic diet reduces intrahepatic triglycerides, decreasing the amount of triglycerides in the liver that can be converted into free fatty acids.
A study published in the Journal of Digestive Diseases Science found that a low-carb diets, including a ketogenic diet, led to histologic improvements in fatty liver conditions.
The study hypothesizes that the improvement in fatty liver conditions is due to reduced fasting glucose and insulin levels, enhancing insulin sensitivity and glucose levels.
Reducing insulin sensitivity and lowering glucose levels decreases the likelihood of de novo lipogenesis, thus reducing the risk of fatty liver conditions.
